title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text: Hi! I’m a clinical biostatistician (PharmD, MPH) documenting my applied technical transition from clinical SAS programming into R fluency, Real-World Evidence (RWE), and Health Economics and Outcomes Research (HEOR) methodologies. This space is my knowledge vault!

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
What I write about


📘 SAS → R
R fundamentals, tidyverse, pharmaverse, and side-by-side translations of SAS patterns I use every day.


🔬 HEOR & RWE
Causal inference, statistical models, study design, data sources, and tools used in real-world evidence.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text: I attended my first ISPOR 2026 conference in Philadelphia. I enrolled in two short courses and attended one main conference day. It was a lot, in the best way. Here’s what I took away and what I’d tell anyone heading there next time.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
section: Go in with a clear goal
text:
Go in with a clear goal
ISPOR is massive. Without a goal, it’s easy to wander. Before you go, identify the area you want to grow in — real-world evidence, comparative effectiveness, causal inference, whatever it is. That focus shapes everything: which sessions you attend, which booths you visit, which conversations you seek out.
For me, I’m actively building toward HEOR and RWE, so I chose my short courses around that:

Causal Inference and Causal Diagrams in Big, Real-World Observational Data and Pragmatic Trials
Developing Decision-Grade Real-World Evidence

Both aligned directly with where I want to grow. Being in a room full of people working on the same problems made it immediately clear who I wanted to talk to. Your goal doesn’t have to be complicated — just intentional.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text:
Network intentionally, not aggressively
Set a realistic target — 3 to 5 genuine conversations. Not just LinkedIn connections. Conversations.
Poster sessions are underrated for this. Find research that interests you, walk up, ask the presenter about their work. People love talking about their research. Connect on LinkedIn while you’re still standing there.
If you’re interested in a specific company, visit their booth — ideally in the morning before it gets crowded. Ask for methodology folks if you want to understand their approach. Ask for HR if you’re exploring opportunities. Be direct, be friendly.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text:
Plan before you arrive
Download the ISPOR app — it’s genuinely useful. Before the conference:

Finalize your short courses and plenary sessions
Browse the attendee list and flag interesting profiles
Review the exhibitor list and make a priority list of booths to visit

Keep only your planned sessions in the app so you’re not distracted scrolling through everything else.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text:
Use your in-person time wisely
Most sessions are recorded. Your competitive advantage at the conference is being there — hands-on workshops, live debates, poster conversations, hallway chats. Prioritize the things that don’t translate to a recording.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text:
Practical things that actually matter

Wear comfortable shoes. Seriously. The walking is brutal.
Bring protein bars and water. You’ll thank yourself.
Don’t overdo it. One focused day beats three burned-out ones.
Talk to people. Everyone is approachable. You never know where a conversation leads.
Write notes immediately. By evening of day two you’ll barely remember day one.

title: My First ISPOR: What Nobody Tells You Before You Go
text:
What I’m taking back

The field is smaller than it looks. The same names appear on posters, in session chairs, in the hallways. Worth knowing who the key practitioners are early.
Methods I’m studying in isolation feel more grounded now — I can picture the context they’re used in.
ISPOR is not a methods conference. If you go expecting deep statistical methodology talks, you’ll be frustrated. What’s debated is which methods, when, and what the results mean for decision-making. That’s a different mode of thinking.







Tip



If you’re transitioning from clinical trials into HEOR/RWE: Go to ISPOR before you feel ready. The exposure to the full evidence-to-decision pipeline is worth more than you expect.

title: About
text:
I’m ShivaPriya Bodduluri — a clinical biostatistician with a PharmD and MPH, currently transitioning from SAS programming into R fluency and HEOR/RWE methodology.
My background is in clinical trials and real-world database research, including experience with CPRD. This site is where I document what I’m learning — partly as a personal reference, partly for anyone walking a similar path.
What I’m building toward: a career at the intersection of real-world evidence, causal inference, and health economics.
